md: Take two numbers, say $12$ and $18$. Their greatest common divisor is $\gcd(12,18) = 6$. Their least common multiple is $\operatorname{lcm}(12,18) = 36$.
Now multiply those two results: $6 \times 36 = 216$. And the two original numbers? $12 \times 18 = 216$. **Same number.** This works for *every* pair, here's why.

blocks.title: The claim
blocks.body: For any two positive whole numbers, $\;\gcd(a,b)\cdot\operatorname{lcm}(a,b) = a\cdot b.$

md: ### The key idea: look at one prime at a time
Every whole number breaks into prime factors. Explore the factor trees of $12$ and $18$:
Here's how GCD and LCM are *built* from these, prime by prime:
- the **GCD** takes the **smaller** exponent of each prime (the most you can share),
- the **LCM** takes the **larger** exponent of each prime (the least that covers both).
### The one fact that finishes it
Look at any single prime, with exponents $x$ and $y$ in the two numbers. The GCD uses $\min(x,y)$ and the LCM uses $\max(x,y)$. But for *any* two numbers:
(One of them is the smaller, the other the larger, together they're just the two values, in some order.)

When you **multiply** $\gcd \times \operatorname{lcm}$, the exponents of each prime **add**. So each prime ends up with exponent $\min + \max = x + y$, which is exactly the exponent that prime has in $a \times b$.

blocks.title: Why this is useful
blocks.body: It gives a shortcut: once you know the GCD (easy to find with the Euclidean algorithm), the LCM is free, $\operatorname{lcm}(a,b) = \dfrac{a\,b}{\gcd(a,b)}$. No need to list multiples at all.
